title: HVAC Electrical Troubleshooting Knowledge Base (Safety-First)
intended_use: AI assistant guidance for homeowners and HVAC technicians. Prioritizes life-safety, prevents unsafe DIY, and provides practical diagnostics for qualified professionals.

assistant_behavior: If the user mentions burning smell, smoke, sparking/arcing, melted insulation, water near electrical equipment, repeated breaker trips, or any shock hazard: STOP troubleshooting, deliver a safety callout, and route to 911/electrician/HVAC tech as appropriate.
Never provide step-by-step instructions for homeowners that involve opening electrical compartments, touching wiring, measuring live voltage, replacing capacitors/contactors, or any panel work.
When uncertain whether the user is a homeowner or technician, assume homeowner and use the strictest safety posture.
For technicians, include PPE/lockout and safe test sequencing before any measurement guidance.

electrical_safety_critical.headline_warning: HVAC equipment contains lethal voltage (120/240V) and stored energy (capacitors) that can shock even when power is off. Electrical work can cause electrocution, arc flash, or fire.
lethal_voltage_warnings: Outdoor condensers and air handlers commonly contain 240V circuits
Control circuits are typically 24V but share cabinets with high voltage
Capacitors can hold charge after power is disconnected
Water + electricity is a life-safety hazard
prohibited_for_homeowners: Opening service panels on outdoor units, air handlers, furnaces, or electrical disconnects
Measuring voltage, amperage, or capacitance with a meter
Replacing capacitors, contactors, transformers, motors, control boards, fuses (inside equipment)
Working inside the breaker panel or replacing breakers
Bypassing safety switches, fuses, or interlocks
Operating equipment with burning smell, smoke, sparking, or water intrusion
technician_ppe_required: Safety glasses and insulated gloves appropriate for task
Arc-rated PPE as required by site safety policy
Insulated tools in energized testing scenarios
Proper footwear and stable work surface
lockout_tagout_procedure: Identify all power sources (main breaker, equipment disconnect, air handler breaker)
Shut off power using disconnects/breakers (both indoor and outdoor as applicable)
Apply lock and tag per company/site policy
Verify de-energized state with an appropriate meter (prove the meter before/after)
Only then begin component inspection or replacement

call_911: Strong electrical burning smell with visible smoke or arcing
call_electrician: Breaker panel issues (buzzing, arcing, scorch marks)
Repeated breaker trips that resume immediately after reset
Burning smell from panel/disconnect/wiring outside HVAC equipment
Lights in house flicker when AC starts
call_hvac_tech: Compressor issues, refrigerant-related faults, control board faults
Low-voltage control problems inside HVAC equipment
Unit hums but fan won't spin

types.run_capacitor: Helps motor/compressor run efficiently and start reliably (PSC motors, some compressors)
types.start_capacitor: Provides extra starting torque for some compressors/motors (often with potential relay or start kit)
types.dual_run_capacitor: Single can that serves both compressor (HERM) and condenser fan (FAN) plus common (C)

ratings.voltage_rule: 440VAC-rated capacitors can replace 370VAC if capacitance matches; reverse is NOT acceptable

symptoms_of_failure: Outdoor unit hums but fan or compressor does not start
Fan blade can be spun and then it runs (classic weak run cap - DO NOT advise homeowners to do this)
Hard starting, intermittent starts, or rapid cycling on overload
why_homeowners_must_not_replace: Shock risk from stored charge and nearby high-voltage terminals
Incorrect wiring can destroy motors/boards or create fire hazards
A failed capacitor may be symptom of deeper issue (motor/compressor over-amps, failing bearings, voltage problems)
Capacitor discharge requires proper resistor-based method - screwdriver shorting is dangerous

contactors.function: High-voltage relay that uses 24V control power to energize the compressor and condenser fan circuit
signs_of_failure: Contacts welded closed (unit runs continuously even when thermostat is OFF)
Chattering/buzzing (weak coil voltage or failing coil)
Ants or debris caught between contacts (preventing closure)
Melted coil casing or burnt smell
common_causes_of_failure: Age and contact erosion from normal arcing
Ants/insects and debris (very common in some regions)
High current draw from failing compressor or fan motor
Low-voltage control issues causing coil chatter
contactors.replacement_notes: Match coil voltage (typically 24VAC) and contact rating. Inspect for upstream causes (over-amps, voltage issues). Technician only.

types.PSC: Permanent Split Capacitor - Older, fixed speed, relies on run capacitor, speed taps
types.ECM: Electronically Commutated Motor - Variable speed, high efficiency, has internal module, not capacitor-based same way

nameplate_terminology.RLA: Rated Load Amps - Compressor running current rating reference
nameplate_terminology.LRA: Locked Rotor Amps - Inrush current at startup (if sustained, motor is seized)
nameplate_terminology.FLA: Full Load Amps - Motor rated current at full load
nameplate_terminology.MCA: Minimum Circuit Ampacity - Used to size the WIRE
nameplate_terminology.MOP: Maximum Overcurrent Protection - Used to size the BREAKER/FUSE

failure_signs: Amp draw exceeds RLA/FLA on nameplate
Motor casing extremely hot to touch
Shaft has play (wiggles) or is hard to turn by hand

save_vs_replace.saveable: Loose wire connection, bad capacitor (motor itself is fine)
save_vs_replace.replace: Bad windings (short to ground/open), seized bearings, ECM module failure, consistent over-amp despite correct voltage and airflow

common_problems: Loose lugs/screws causing resistance = heat = melted wires
Corrosion on terminals (common in coastal areas)
Rub-outs (copper tube vibrating against wire insulation)

breaker_sizing.MCA: Minimum Circuit Ampacity - Used to size the WIRE
breaker_sizing.MOP: Maximum Overcurrent Protection - Used to size the BREAKER/FUSE
common_mistakes: Undersizing breaker leading to nuisance trips
Using breaker size as conductor sizing guide (incorrect)

warning_signs: Scorch marks in disconnect or equipment

transformers_low_voltage.basics: Steps down 120V/240V to 24V for control circuit (thermostat, contactor coil, control board)
common_issues: Blown 3A or 5A fuse on control board
Short in thermostat wire (dogs chewing, weed eater damage, pinched at staples)
Shorted contactor coil or control component
transformers_low_voltage.diagnosis_tip: If thermostat is blank and batteries are good, check condensate float switch first (interrupts 24V). Then check fuse on board. Then transformer.

diagnostic_symptoms_table.symptom: Outdoor unit hums but won't start
diagnostic_symptoms_table.safety_first: Do not touch the unit or attempt manual fan spinning. Shut off at thermostat.
diagnostic_symptoms_table.homeowner_check: Check thermostat settings, check breaker once, clear debris around unit

diagnostic_symptoms_table.safety_first: Reset ONE time only. If trips again, STOP. If burning smell/buzzing/heat at panel: call electrician urgently.
diagnostic_symptoms_table.homeowner_check: One-time reset only. Note WHEN it trips (on start vs after running).
diagnostic_symptoms_table.call_pro_if: Trips again. Electrician if panel symptoms. HVAC tech if equipment suspected.

diagnostic_symptoms_table.safety_first: Do not open the air handler cabinet.
diagnostic_symptoms_table.homeowner_check: Check thermostat fan setting (AUTO/ON), replace filter, check breaker once

diagnostic_symptoms_table.symptom: System won't turn on at all
diagnostic_symptoms_table.safety_first: If burning smell or water near equipment: stop and call pro.
likely_causes: Transformer failure or blown low-voltage fuse
diagnostic_symptoms_table.homeowner_check: Thermostat batteries and settings, breaker reset ONE time, check for water around drain pan (without opening panels)
diagnostic_symptoms_table.call_pro_if: Power returns then fails again, water overflow present, breaker trips again

diagnostic_symptoms_table.safety_first: Turn system OFF immediately at thermostat. If smoke/sparking/flames: LEAVE and call 911. Do not reset breakers.
diagnostic_symptoms_table.homeowner_check: NONE beyond turning system off and reporting observations

rules: No touching internal components, wiring, or opening service panels
If unsure, stop and call a professional

safe_checks.steps: Identify HVAC breaker, switch fully OFF then ON once. If trips again, stop and call pro.
safe_checks.stop_if: Any burning smell at panel, buzzing/arcing, heat at breaker

safe_checks.steps: Confirm mode (COOL/HEAT), setpoint, schedule. Replace batteries if applicable.
Look for water in pan under indoor unit (without opening panels). If full, vacuum out drain line from outside termination.
Ensure filter is not clogged, which can overheat motors and trip safeties.

what_to_report_to_technician: Exact symptom and timing (on startup vs after running)
Any recent power outage or lightning event
Any code displayed on thermostat/control board
Whether breaker trips immediately or after a few minutes
Photos of outdoor unit placement and any visible damage (from safe distance)

systematic_approach: Start with safety: PPE, LOTO, verify de-energized before opening compartments
Confirm complaint and operating mode; check thermostat call and 24V control integrity
Inspect visually for heat damage, loose connections, debris, moisture, component condition
Verify supply voltage under load; confirm disconnect and breaker integrity
Check control voltage (24VAC) to contactor/relays; verify safeties in series
Test capacitors (after safe discharge), contactor coil resistance, contact condition
Measure running amps and compare to nameplate; watch for abnormal inrush/locked rotor
Document findings and root cause, not just the failed part

meter_readings_guide.prove_your_meter: Verify meter on known live source before testing, test circuit, verify again after

common_patterns.reading: Low line voltage under load + high current draw
common_patterns.indicates: Supply issue, loose connection, undersized conductors, utility sag

common_patterns.reading: Contactor coil energized but contacts not closing fully
common_patterns.indicates: Weak coil voltage, failing coil, mechanical binding/debris
Failed capacitor - replace with correct µF and equal/higher voltage rating
Weak capacitor/start kit issue, locked rotor compressor, low voltage

documentation_best_practices: Record nameplate data (MCA/MOP, RLA/LRA/FLA) and measured values with conditions
Note root cause suspects (ants in contactor, loose lug causing heat, etc.)
Provide customer summary: what failed, why, what was done, how to prevent recurrence
